TextENGNew: ``Yeah.@k@vS06/03/150300058. His death was definitely sudden.``

``The priest had been overworked preparing for Watanagashi, and he'd apparently been under the weather for a while.@k@vS06/03/150300060. It was something like acute heart failure -- a sudden medical condition. Nothing suspicious about it, supposedly.``
changeReason: Old condenses the description, omitting 急性心不全 ("acute heart failure"), the priest being overworked preparing for Watanagashi, and Mion's personal opinion qualifier; new includes these details from the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``But it's insane that someone died on Watanagashi again.@k@vS06/06/150600207. Three years in a row.@k@vS06/06/150600208. Normally, you'd think the chances of someone dying on a specific date three consecutive years are astronomically low.``
``The police performed an autopsy, and since they didn't make a fuss afterward, there must not have been anything suspicious about the cause of death.``
``...But you're right -- when deaths pile up three years running, all on the same day of Watanagashi... it's hard to call that normal.``
changeReason: Old summarizes as "the whole situation is abnormal" while the Japanese and new translation specify "deaths piling up three years running on the same day of Watanagashi" -- significant contextual detail omitted.

TextENGNew: ``And like you just said, Sis -- it's only the people who'd be most likely targets of the curse who keep dying.@k@vS06/06/150600210. There has to be something behind this.@k@vS06/06/150600211. There absolutely has to be.``
changeReason: Old omits the reference to what Mion previously said (さっきお姉も言った通り) and drops the "conspiracy or curse" dichotomy (祟りでも陰謀でも) present in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: I arranged to meet Mion at the library near Okinomiya Station.
...It wasn't that I didn't trust Mion, but there was no reason to broadcast my address unnecessarily.
This sparsely attended library saw few visitors from town, and even fewer from Hinamizawa.
That made it an ideal spot for us to meet without being seen.
After thoroughly going over our living arrangements, my job prospects, and how we'd coordinate as sisters, we settled into idle chatter.
I'd been away from this town for only about a year.
But a year is more than enough for things to change.
...I pumped Mion for information on all the various changes, trying to recover that lost year.
And among the many things we discussed... the conversation eventually circled back to what Uncle Yoshiroh had told me at our meeting.
The story of Oyashiro-sama's curse.
Even before I left town...
The curse had already struck two years running.
The construction foreman's murder in the first year had been shocking enough.
And then the following year--

The dam project's freeze had just been announced, and Hinamizawa was still flush with victory when Watanagashi arrived.@k That was when the pro-dam Houjou couple fell to their deaths in an 'accident.'
The impact of that was enormous.
Divine punishment fell upon the dam site foreman who had defiled Hinamizawa, and then upon the village traitors who had backed the project.
...If it had happened only once, people could still call Oyashiro-sama's curse a coincidence -- just an unfortunate incident.
But when a second year followed the same pattern... the mood shifted to: could this be... no, surely not... is the curse actually real?@k That was the prevailing atmosphere.
That was as much as I knew.
And while I was away from town,@k the third year had brought yet another incident.
In both Hinamizawa and Okinomiya, talk of Oyashiro-sama's curse now circulated with an intensity that dwarfed anything from before.
...I needed to completely revise my understanding.
During my absence, Oyashiro-sama's curse had acquired an overwhelming sense of reality.
Up through the second year, it had still been a question: 'Could this possibly be the curse?'
But now, after three consecutive years, it had hardened into near-certainty: 'This can only be the curse.'
And the fact that the priest who died in the third year, while not exactly an enemy of the village,@k had been viewed unfavorably by the villagers... only bolstered the theory's credibility.
``Wasn't the priest... rather disliked?@k@vS06/06/150600213. By the protest alliance leaders, I mean.``

``Yeah.@k@vS06/03/150300064. They criticized him for being a fence-sitter.@k@vS06/03/150300065. Toward the end of the conflict, the alliance grew increasingly radical, and it split into hardliner and moderate factions... It got pretty ugly.``
The priest had a stern face and a somewhat rigid manner of speaking -- he certainly looked intimidating at first glance.
But in reality, he was an extraordinarily gentle, reserved gentleman.
That reserve was probably partly because the priest had married into the family, as an adopted son-in-law.
As the caretaker of a shrine that served as both the summer festival venue and the village's communal gathering place, a personality like his may actually have been the perfect fit.

But that easygoing, neighborhood-gathering-spot atmosphere of the shrine@k transformed utterly the moment it became the headquarters of the Onigafuchi Guardians -- the anti-dam resistance organization formed the instant the Dam War erupted.
Before the Dam War, Oyashiro-sama hadn't received all that much worship.
The devout elderly, of course, paid their respects. But for the average person, Oyashiro-sama was treated with the same casual regard as any local deity -- believe it or don't, your call.
Then the Dam War began, and this previously unremarkable deity was suddenly hoisted up as the symbol of the village's resistance.
Tracing the Oyashiro-sama faith back to its roots,@k it was an insular, exclusionary tradition born from the desire to protect one's homeland -- and to reject outsiders who would trample upon it.
Of course, such parochial ideology was a relic of the distant past, utterly untenable in peaceful postwar Japan.
And so it was a doctrine that had rightly faded with the times.

But the ideology had never truly died. It was merely dormant, and was dug back up and resurrected when the need arose to rally the community against the dam project, fanning the flames of local nationalism.
Once those ideas were revived, events could only spiral in an ever more radical direction.
...It took no time at all for the Onigafuchi Guardians to take on an aggressive character.
After that, it was essentially a wartime footing.
All hearts as one, united in a single purpose. Now is the time to defend our village, even at the cost of our lives--.
Adults and children alike bristled with determination to repel the outside enemy, nostrils flaring with righteous fury.
...Described like this, it probably conjures up something deeply unpleasant -- a backward, insular atmosphere that smacks of darker chapters in history.
But as I mentioned before, for us children it felt less like a war and more like playing at war -- a festival in which the entire village united as one.
...Yet festival or not, anyone who voiced dissent was met with open hostility.
...Their aggressive nature fully lived up to the 'Defend to the Death' in the alliance's name.
changeReason: Old mistranslates 死守 ("defend to the death") as "wartime regime"; new correctly references the Onigafuchi Death-Defend Alliance's name.

TextENGNew: Looking back, the Houjou couple -- who publicly declared their acceptance of the dam project amid all of this -- were... genuinely courageous.
And when they died in an 'accident' during the curse's second year,@k not a soul doubted it was Oyashiro-sama's doing.
changeReason: Old is an entirely different sentence ("branded traitors") while the Japanese says the Sonozaki head declared the deaths 因果応報 ("karmic retribution/just deserts") -- a completely different meaning.

TextENGNew: The head of the Sonozaki family went so far as to openly declare it 'just deserts'...
changeReason: Old translates a different meaning ("faced worse than just being ostracized") while the Japanese describes the Sonozaki hatred as extreme from the narrator's own perspective (こちら側の私から見ても過激).

TextENGNew: The Sonozaki family's hatred toward the Houjou couple was extreme -- even from my perspective, as someone on their side.
changeReason: Old adds "talked behind their backs" not present in the Japanese (黙殺するかたち = "tacitly endorsing/ignoring"); new correctly renders the village atmosphere as silently endorsing the ostracism.

TextENGNew: Naturally, the village atmosphere followed suit, silently endorsing the ostracism.
The only person in the entire village who didn't treat the Houjou couple as enemies... was the priest.

While the priest never spoke against the anti-dam movement,@k he also never condemned the Houjou couple's stance -- that accepting relocation and receiving government compensation to rebuild their lives was a valid choice.
For the Three Families (especially the Sonozakis), who had been rallying village unity by making 'Never forgive the Houjous!' their rallying cry and scapegoating the couple,

the fact that a fellow member of the Three Families -- and moreover, the priest of the very shrine that enshrined Oyashiro-sama, the symbol of the anti-dam resistance -- had tacitly condoned them... was deeply galling.

The Three Families were the backbone of the Onigafuchi Guardians and the pillars of Hinamizawa Village; disunity among them was unacceptable.@k Not wanting to stir internal conflict, they refrained from openly censuring the priest.
...But behind closed doors, people whispered that the priest was a heretic, unfit to serve Oyashiro-sama's shrine,@k and that surely Oyashiro-sama's punishment would find him sooner or later...
``Looking back on it now, don't you think the priest was actually the mature one?``
In an environment where everyone was drunk on fervor, keeping his cool and maintaining neutrality took real courage. That might have been something to admire.
I expected Mion to agree with me, to nod along in shared understanding.
...So when Mion responded with a strained smile... and didn't share my view... I was a little taken aback.

``...Hmm...@k@vS06/03/150300068. If he'd truly been mature, I think that was a situation where he should have gone along with everyone else and kept the peace.@k@vS06/03/150300069. ...That's just what I think.``
changeReason: Old softens 確執 (discord/feud/antagonism) to "disagreements" while new's "resentment" better captures the adversarial connotation of the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``At a time when all of Hinamizawa needed to stand united, for a member of the Three Families -- who should have been setting the example -- to voluntarily break ranks...@k@vS06/03/150300071. Can you really call that 'mature'?``
I was momentarily at a loss for words.
All I managed was this:
Even though we're mirror-image twins, Mion and Shion each possessed her own distinct personality.
Occasionally, our views would differ.
But those differences were usually trivial, inconsequential things.
A gap in opinion this wide... had never happened before.
The disconnect unsettled me. I decided to probe the difference between Mion's position and mine.
``I'm not sure I like that word you used -- 'keep the peace.'@k@vS06/06/150600218. What exactly do you mean by that?``

``Are you saying the priest should have hidden his true feelings for the sake of the village?@k@vS06/06/150600220. That in a collectivist environment, the individual shouldn't speak up? Is that what you're telling me?``

``...I-I'm not saying it that strongly...@k@vS06/03/150300073. Just that... as one of the Three Family heads, maybe he should have been a bit more careful with his words and actions... that's all I meant...``
My tone had apparently grown interrogative, because Mion answered with a hint of flustered defensiveness.
I realized I'd been speaking more harshly than I'd intended.
...Mion's perspective was probably the more 'mature' one, by the measure we'd been using.
When facing a colossal enemy like the national government and everyone needed to rise together, you shouldn't do anything to pour cold water on that unity.
...Written out and read back like this, it isn't an unreasonable position.
But I simply wasn't mature enough to nod along with such collectivist thinking.
No... saying that I wasn't mature isn't quite right.
...Perhaps the more accurate way to put it is that Mion had adopted a mature way of thinking.
...It had been just over a year since we sisters were definitively separated into Mion and Shion.
During that time, I'd been isolated at the academy, cut off from any connection to this town.
changeReason: Old reduces 学園に隔離され、町との接点を空白にしていた ("isolated at the academy, cut off from any connection to town") to just "I've been away from the town," losing the sense of enforced isolation.

TextENGNew: ...But meanwhile, Mion had been studying under the head of the Sonozaki family, learning the arts of leadership as the next heir.
Of course, even before I was confined, Mion had already been treated as the heir apparent.
She was made to sit beside our grandmother at every ceremony and gathering, and when Grandmother was absent, she'd recite greetings on her behalf -- that sort of role.
changeReason: Old says Mion "took her place when the hag was absent" implying full substitution, while the Japanese specifies a more limited role: 挨拶文を代読する ("reciting greetings on her behalf").

TextENGNew: But that had been the extent of it.
It hadn't created a gap large enough for our opinions to diverge.
In this last year or so... Mion had become this much of a 'next family head.'
``...I see.@k@vS06/06/150600222. Sis, you've really grown up without me noticing.``
Mion seemed to understand what I was feeling and what I was implying.
``...Am I starting to think like Granny now...?``
``That's a good thing.@k@vS06/06/150600224. You're the next family head, after all.@k@vS06/06/150600225. You can't keep thinking like common folk forever.``
changeReason: New omits the final line about 帝王学 ("the art of rulership/statecraft") present in both the Japanese and the old translation.

TextENGNew: ``I haven't learned anything special.@k@vS06/03/150300076. All I've gotten better at is imitating Granny... that's it...``
``You could do it just as easily.``
``Ahahahahaha, no thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600228. Babysitting the old hag is your job, Mion.@k@vS06/06/150600229. I'll keep cheering you on from the sidelines, safe and sound!``
changeReason: Old omits バァさんのお守り ("babysitting the old hag") and the specific dismissive framing; new captures the teasing tone of the original more faithfully.

TextENGNew: I hadn't meant to sound dismissive, but it seemed to come across that way to Mion.
She wore a placating, almost ingratiating smile.
I softened my expression.
``...Wh-... what is it?``

``Our separations have never been gentle, have they?@k@vS06/06/150600233. Our names were torn apart, our homes were torn apart... and when we ourselves were physically separated, it was never a peaceful parting either.``
Mion's expression darkened, and she looked down.@k ...Then she peered up at me from beneath lowered lashes, reading my face.
``...You might think I hold a grudge over all that.@k@vS06/06/150600233_2. But that's a misunderstanding.@k@vS06/06/150600233_3. Sure, I've felt flashes of resentment here and there.``
changeReason: Old translates 逆恨み ("holding a grudge/resentment") as "jealous," which conveys a different emotional concept; new's "hold a grudge" is more accurate.

TextENGNew: ``But those feelings wash away with time.@k@vS06/06/150600233_5. You have nothing to feel guilty about.``
We had been perfectly equal up until the moment we emerged from our mother's womb.
The instant we were born, the outside world imposed an arbitrary hierarchy upon us.
...There were times when that injustice kindled anger in a child's heart.@k I won't deny it anymore.
But there was no reason for such old wounds to keep driving a wedge between us sisters.
Mion seemed to grasp what I was trying to say, if only barely.
...We sisters are each other's mirror images, but of course we have our own distinct traits.
Traits that only the two of us know about.
...Among them was our degree of dependency on each other.
Compared to me, Mion's dependency on the other was far higher.
...She was the type who worried about me constantly and held back out of consideration.
If Mion alone was given a bun, she'd break it in half and bring the other piece to me.
If she was given a single piece of candy, she'd watch my face and wouldn't put it in her mouth until I gave my approval.
I, on the other hand, was cold.
If I stood to benefit alone, I didn't spare a thought for the other person.
I'd pop it into my mouth right away with a brisk 'thanks for the food.'
(Of course, this is all predicated on the deep sisterly love we share.@k Let me reiterate -- I don't dislike my sister.)
...We both knew and accepted these things about each other.
The heir's position was a piece of candy that couldn't be split in two... and Mion must have spent all this time watching my face, wondering whether she was allowed to put it in her mouth.
``Do your best... Mion.@k@vS06/06/150600235. I'll do my best as Shion, too.``
I pressed my clenched fist gently against Mion's forehead.
...Mion held her forehead against my fist, still and quiet, as if absorbing some minute warmth that could only be felt through touch.
This one is Mion.
And I am Shion.
She is the next head of the Sonozaki family. I am a free spirit with no idea where tomorrow's wind will blow.
changeReason: Old translates 自由人 ("free person") as "vagabond" (negative/homeless connotation) and omits 明日はどこ吹く風 ("wherever tomorrow's wind blows"); new captures the carefree spirit of the Japanese.

TextENGNew: That isn't discrimination. It's individuality.
Let's stop fretting over each other's lot in life.@k Let's each embrace our own identity and live with our shoulders unburdened...
None of this needed to be spoken aloud.@k Through this simple contact, the message was conveyed.
``We've got this, Mion.@k@vS06/06/150600237. If anything's troubling you, come talk to me anytime.@k@vS06/06/150600238. We can always be there for each other.``
Mion kept nodding, pushing her forehead against my fist, her gaze cast downward...
Mion and I parted before evening came.
I'd recovered all the information I'd missed during my absence, and we'd thoroughly enjoyed our long-overdue sisterly heart-to-heart.
...And we'd managed to untangle the knots that had been sitting in both our hearts.
In the past, we'd never have had that kind of conversation.
Perhaps the year apart had made it possible.
Late afternoon, just before evening.
...With slightly lighter shoulders, I stretched wide and gazed up at the sky.
The clear sky stretched on endlessly -- blue and impossibly far away.
No matter how far I, a sapling, might stretch my branches, they'd never touch anything up there.@k It was that kind of sky -- vast, high, and immeasurably distant.
changeReason: Old drops the tree/branch metaphor (私という木が、枝を伸ばしても = "even if the tree that is me stretches its branches") from the Japanese, losing the poetic imagery.

TextENGNew: I am Sonozaki Shion.
...A free spirit who fled the life that was forced upon her.
I may be condemned for it, but my future will never be one that wins anyone's praise.
...But it's a life I chose for myself.
``Ahh!@k@vS06/06/150600242. ...Look at that -- I can think like a grown-up too.``
I rallied my own spirits, trying to put some fire in my step.
With renewed energy, I decided to go do some grocery shopping for dinner.
Kasai had fronted me enough for living expenses in the short term, but I still needed to spend carefully.
Junk food was tempting, but if I wanted to save money, cooking for myself was unavoidable.
...Ironically, the home economics classes drilled into me at the academy were going to prove useful.
Maybe I'd head over to the Kami-isshiki shopping district.
...Wait.@k If I'm going there, this isn't the right street.@k It should be a bit shorter from the other direction.
The late-afternoon breeze, just beginning to carry a hint of coolness, felt wonderful on my skin.
Kasai showed up while I was unpacking my groceries and loading the fridge.
``Ah -- hang on, hang on.@k@vS06/06/150600242_2. Let me get the lock.``
``...Just back from shopping?@k@vS06/15/151500091. You must be tired.``
``Tired from a little grocery run? Hardly! Ahahaha.@k@vS06/06/150600244. Oh, want to stay for dinner, Kasai?@k@vS06/06/150600245. You live alone, right? I doubt you cook much for yourself.``
``Are you sure?@k@vS06/15/151500093. ...If you're offering, I'd be delighted to.``
Kasai wore a look of amused curiosity, clearly wondering what kind of meal was about to materialize.
``Hmph.@k@vS06/06/150600247. That face says you don't believe in me at all, does it?@k@vS06/06/150600248. Just you watch~!``
``Shall I put these in the fridge?@k@vS06/15/151500095. I'll take care of it.``
``Oh, thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600250. ...For two people, this much rice should be enough, right?@k@vS06/06/150600251. Hup we go.``
Kasai examined the mountain of groceries, expertly sorting them into the refrigerator as he went.
Then something caught his eye, and he spoke up.
``...Shion-san.@k@vS06/15/151500097. Still can't do canned food?``
He didn't need to point it out -- not a single can was among my purchases.@k ...That was no coincidence. It was deliberate.
``Shopping while avoiding canned goods must make things rather difficult.``
``Mmm...@k@vS06/06/150600253. I know it's irrational...@k@vS06/06/150600254. ...If someone forced me, I could probably choke it down. But willingly? No thanks.``
``I do feel bad about that.@k@vS06/15/151500100. I know I took the joke too far.@k@vS06/15/151500101. So really, it's about time you got over it... I'd feel better.``
``Ahhh, enough already!@k@vS06/06/150600257. Men shouldn't be poking around the kitchen!@k@vS06/06/150600258. Go watch TV or something!``
Kasai chuckled softly, and without pressing further, obediently retreated.
I know it's silly, even to myself.@k ...But I simply cannot abide canned food.
Even if I eat something without knowing, the moment someone tells me afterward that the meat came from a can, I feel the urge to vomit.
The cause was utterly trivial.
...When I was little, Kasai told me a ghost story that stuck.
...You know the type -- one of those urban legends.
About meat of dubious origin.
Like how the meat in hamburgers isn't really beef, or how the beef at the gyuudon chain has something else mixed in -- that sort of story.
The precocious little me got thoroughly spooked, so Kasai apparently found it hilarious and kept laying it on thick.
Great fun for the Kasai of those days, but a serious problem for the me of those days.
I developed such an aversion to canned food that I'd burst into tears in the canned goods aisle of the supermarket.
Of course, I no longer react that extremely...@k but I still don't like them.
...And since I don't like them, there's no reason to force myself. These days, I naturally steer around canned goods as a matter of course.
Incidentally, this is unique to me.@k Mion doesn't share it.
Kasai does seem to feel somewhat responsible, but beyond sticking his tongue out apologetically, there's not much he can do about it.
Maybe someone as perfect as me needs at least one little quirk like this to keep things interesting.@k Ahahahahahahaha.
``Kasai~, clear the newspapers off the table~.``
``Smells wonderful.@k@vS06/15/151500103. Thank you for the meal.``
``If it's not enough food, you can always hit up a gyuudon place afterward.@k@vS06/06/150600261. Oh, and leave the TV on.@k@vS06/06/150600262. There's a drama I want to watch.``
``Well then -- itadakimasu.``
``So?@k@vS06/06/150600264. Kasai, what did you actually come here for?@k@vS06/06/150600265. You came for a reason, didn't you?``
I asked while passing him a tea bowl.
I could have waited until after dinner, but I've never had the patience for roundabout approaches.
``...I was planning to bring it up after the meal, but... shall we talk over dinner?``
``It's been nagging at me since you walked in.@k@vS06/06/150600267. Better to just say it -- the food will go down easier once it's off my mind.``
``The school called the house today.``
``Today?@k@vS06/06/150600269. Ahahaha, that took them long enough.@k@vS06/06/150600270. They really didn't want this going public, did they?``
``Akane-san took the call.@k@vS06/15/151500108. She said she'll tell your father when the time is right.``
'Akane-san' refers to my mother.@k It's how Kasai addresses her -- a holdover from when they knew each other before my parents married.
``And?@k@vS06/06/150600274. Did Mom say anything?``
It was lucky that Mom had been the one to answer.
She's relatively sympathetic to my cause.
``She muttered 'what a hopeless child'...@k@vS06/15/151500110. ...but the look on her face said this outcome wasn't entirely unexpected.``
``...I see.@k@vS06/06/150600276. She hasn't told Dad yet?``
``Akane-san is surely picking her moment carefully.@k@vS06/15/151500112. I'd expect her to tell him within the next two or three days.``
``...Dad's in Granny's camp, so...@k@vS06/06/150600278. Once he hears, he'll automatically pass it on to her.@k@vS06/06/150600279. ...Which means things are about to get interesting.``
``Depending on how my explanation goes, it could cost me a finger or two...@k@vS06/06/150600281. ...How terrifying.@k@vS06/06/150600282. Ahahahaha!``
``I'd be glad if you could laugh this off,@k@vS06/15/151500114. but... please do keep in mind that it won't be just your fingers on the line.@k@vS06/15/151500115. Heh heh heh.``
``Ahahahaha!@k@vS06/06/150600284. You're wolfing it down!@k@vS06/06/150600285. Oh, and I made way too much miso soup and rice -- have seconds. Have thirds!``
A few days later, Mion reported that word of my escape had reached our grandmother.
``So?@k@vS06/06/150600288. How did Granny take it?``
``She was absolutely livid.@k@vS06/03/150300090. Said that if I'm anywhere in the Okinomiya area, she wants me found and dragged before her immediately.``
``Yikes.@k@vS06/06/150600290. And?@k@vS06/06/150600291. What's Dad actually doing about it?``

``Sounds like they'll question whether anyone's sheltering you at the next family council.@k@vS06/03/150300092. But it doesn't seem like they're going to send people to comb the streets or anything.``
The family council questioning was mainly for show -- to satisfy Grandmother.
The fact that they had no intention of conducting a thorough search essentially amounted to a tacit acceptance of my presence.
Naturally, if I strutted around in plain sight, I'd be hauled in immediately.
``...Sounds like Mom pulled a lot of strings behind the scenes.``
I could tell she was supporting my chosen way of life in her own way.
...It's a long story, so I'll keep it brief: Mom carries Sonozaki blood, but she's something of a rebel.
Years ago, she apparently despised the Sonozaki family's strict codes and traditions,@k and after a ferocious clash with Grandmother, was actually disowned for a time.
...My mother was originally supposed to be the next family head, but that title was stripped from her. That's why the succession skipped a generation to my sister.
She's quieted down now and avoids making waves, but at her core, Mom is still a maverick.
...That's precisely why she has always taken my side -- someone who also suffers under the Sonozaki family's rigid customs.
``Dad was apparently furious at first.@k@vS06/03/150300094. Mom apparently talked him down quite a bit.@k@vS06/03/150300095. ...Thanks to that, it sounds like they won't be mounting a major search.``

``Got it, thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600294. Let me know if anything else comes up.@k@vS06/06/150600295. ...If things get truly, genuinely dangerous, I'll vanish somewhere they'll never find me. So don't worry too much.``
``...Sis, I can tell what you're thinking right now.@k@vS06/06/150600298. You're asking yourself -- why does Shion, my own flesh-and-blood sister, have to live like a fugitive?``
Mion's silence confirmed it.
``It isn't fair that only you have to live this way...@k@vS06/03/150300098. It just isn't.``
``...Thank you.@k@vS06/06/150600300. Just knowing you feel that way means a lot, Sis.``
``...If you ever want to go out somewhere, tell me.@k@vS06/03/150300100. I'll make sure you can be Mion whenever you need to...``
The apology weighed heavy in her voice.
...At times like this, going awkwardly silent or making sympathetic noises would only drag us deeper into the mire.
Unlike me, Mion isn't the dry, detached type.@k Once she sinks into something, she has a hard time climbing back out.
So I told her simply, without ceremony:
``Thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600302. ...Actually, that's the biggest help of all.@k@vS06/06/150600303. I'll hang up now.``
``Okay. Talk to you later.``
